claim_chain: Accurate infectious disease models require accurate population contact data
Previous contact data do not reflect post-pandemic societal structures or capture socioeconomic and ethnic mixing
We conducted a large representative survey measuring contacts across age, ethnicity, and socioeconomic groups
Contacts decreased ~20% since 2005-2006 but increased 40% since late 2022
Novel finding: contacts are assortative between ethnic groups and socioeconomic classes in all settings
Demographic groups with largest share of infections differ from those with highest per-capita risk
Social structure contributes to inequalities in infectious disease burden through differential contact patterns

typical_pattern: State what the study provides or addresses
Present main quantitative findings with confidence intervals
Interpret implications for disease transmission or public health

likely_priorities: Methodological rigor in contact measurement and classification
Representativeness of sample and response rates
Validity of comparisons to historical POLYMOD data
Handling of missing data and large group contacts
Statistical approach to weighting and modeling
likely_attacks: Self-reported contacts subject to recall bias and reporting fatigue
Contact definition may not capture all transmission-relevant interactions
Age structure of large group contacts inferred from POLYMOD assumptions
Characteristics of contacts may have been estimated by participants
NS-SEC classification is only one definition of socioeconomic status
Missing data on physical interaction for 4% of contacts
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The discussion explicitly lists multiple limitations: 'Our data collection methodology had some limitations' followed by detailed enumeration including recall bias, reporting fatigue, contact definition limitations, inference assumptions about large group contacts, and missing data. The analysis limitations section states 'The data analysis was also subject to limitations' and discusses stratification constraints, missing physical interaction data, and limitations of NS-SEC classification. These self-identified weaknesses form the basis for inferring likely reviewer concerns.

examples.quoted_phrase: to the best of our knowledge, the first data on mixing within and between ethnic and socioeconomic groups
These decreased contact levels suggest that there may be long-term shifts in the epidemiology
Our findings suggest that these risk disparities persist within NS-SEC strata
this study provides quantitative evidence of assortative mixing between ethnic groups and NS-SEC classes in all settings
These results suggest that social structure contributes to inequalities in infectious disease burden

phrase_evidence.phrase: The accuracy and utility of infectious disease transmission models depend on the availability of accurate data on the population in question
Our study provides up-to-date post-pandemic data on social contact patterns in the UK with a large, representative survey sample
to the best of our knowledge, the first data on mixing within and between ethnic and socioeconomic groups
We found that reported contacts have decreased by ~20% since the POLYMOD survey was conducted in 2005–2006
These decreased contact levels suggest that there may be long-term shifts in the epidemiology of a range of close-contact infectious diseases
A key and novel finding of this study relates to contacts within and between ethnic groups and socioeconomic classes
this study provides quantitative evidence of assortative mixing between ethnic groups and NS-SEC classes in all settings
Our analysis suggests that demographic groups projected to have the largest share of infections are not necessarily those with the highest per-capita risk
These results suggest that social structure contributes to inequalities in infectious disease burden through differential contact patterns
The inequalities in exposure risk that our findings quantify are consistent with observations during the COVID-19 pandemic
Our data collection methodology had some limitations
Self-reported contacts may have been subject to recall bias
This definition does not necessarily capture all close proximity contacts
We attempted to minimise the effect of recall bias by recruiting individuals one day ahead of the survey date
The data analysis was also subject to limitations
we do not believe that this is substantially impactful on data analysis
Mean daily number of social contacts and associated 95% confidence intervals
estimated using a negative binomial regression model weighted by participants' age group, gender, ethnicity, and weekday/weekend survey day
All contact matrices estimated by the weighted negative binomial regression model
Mean projected share of infections by demographic groups, with 95% confidence intervals
While contacts were reduced on both weekdays and weekends compared to POLYMOD
Our overdispersion results indicate that there is substantially greater heterogeneity in contact levels post-pandemic
We found that age-assortative contacts in the home are more disassortative by gender in older ages
the prevalence of physical contact was very similar to those found by the POLYMOD survey across all stratifications
This study provides crucial data to inform post-pandemic mathematical models of infectious disease transmission

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. Quantitative values (percentages, confidence intervals, relative risks) are directly quoted from the paper. No external references, author names, or invented data appear in the profile. The reviewer preference profile is inferred solely from the paper's explicit limitations sections.

_phrase_violations.phrase: estimated using a negative binomial regression model weighted by participants' age group, gender, ethnicity, and weekday/weekend survey day
